text.title: Procurement Management Framework Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the procurement-management methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Always include 'build internally' or 'do nothing' as an explicit row in the evaluation matrix.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline comparison, the decision optimises vendor selection rather than whether to engage one at all.

rule.statement: Select contract type by scope clarity: Fixed Price for well-defined scope; T&M for unclear scope with hard cap + 70% trigger; Cost Plus only for research with full buyer risk acceptance.
rule.rationale: Mismatched contract type is the primary cause of cost overruns and disputes; variable scope on fixed-price guarantees conflict.

rule.statement: Publish the scoring rubric and weights before proposals arrive. Retroactive weight changes are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Pre-published weights force the evaluating team to commit to priorities before they are influenced by vendor positioning.

rule.statement: Every contract must pass the clause checklist before signing: IP assignment, sub-processor list, DPA (if PII), termination-for-convenience with notice, audit right, SLA with credits, indemnity (incl. AI output IP), warranty period. No 'TBD' clauses.
rule.rationale: Missing clauses surface as disputes; an undiscovered IP gap locks the buyer into a bad vendor indefinitely.

rule.statement: Tie payments to acceptance of deliverables, not calendar dates. Default Fixed Price split: 25% start, 50% milestone, 25% acceptance.
rule.rationale: Calendar payments reward timing; milestone-gated payments preserve leverage for final acceptance.

rule.statement: After scoring, re-score with each criterion weight shifted ±10%. If rank order flips, document the rationale or re-evaluate.
rule.rationale: A rank that changes under small weight variations means the choice is essentially arbitrary; false precision hides this.

list.item: Spot purchases under PO threshold (e.g. under $5K) where formal procurement is overhead
Highly regulated industries with established central procurement and legal teams — defer to org policy
SaaS tools the engineering team can self-onboard with monthly billing (security review still needed, but not a full RFP)
